past_medical_history: Generalized anxiety disorder, diagnosed 6 years ago
Major depressive episode at age 28, treated with psychotherapy and medication
Irritable bowel syndrome, managed with dietary modifications
No prior neurological diagnoses or hospitalizations
family_history: Father with type 2 diabetes and hypertension
No known family history of epilepsy or movement disorders

social_history.occupation: Administrative assistant at a law firm; reports high workplace stress over the past year due to staffing changes
social_history.alcohol: Social drinker, 1-2 glasses of wine on weekends
social_history.tobacco: Former smoker, quit 4 years ago, approximately 5 pack-year history
social_history.recreational_drugs: Denies current use; remote history of marijuana use in college
social_history.living_situation: Lives with her partner of 7 years in a rented apartment; no children
social_history.trauma_history: Reports a difficult childhood with parental divorce at age 10; acknowledges this has been a topic in past therapy sessions but declines to elaborate further
social_history.exercise: Previously active with running and swimming; has been largely sedentary over the past 6 months due to symptoms

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Speech fluent with normal rate and prosody. No word-finding difficulty or paraphasic errors. Naming, repetition, and comprehension intact. Recall 3/3 at registration and 3/3 at 5 minutes. Affect is flat with intermittent tearfulness when discussing symptom severity. Attention intact on serial sevens and digit span (6 forward, 4 backward).
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal and reactive, 3 mm to 2 mm bilaterally. Visual fields full to confrontation. Extraocular movements intact without nystagmus. Facial sensation intact in all three divisions bilaterally. Facial symmetry at rest and with activation. Hearing grossly intact bilaterally. Palate elevates symmetrically. Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius strength 5/5 bilaterally. Tongue midline without fasciculations.
neurological_exam.motor: Bulk normal throughout. Tone normal in upper extremities bilaterally; no rigidity or spasticity. In lower extremities, the patient demonstrates give-way weakness in bilateral hip flexors and knee extensors, with strength collapsing from 4/5 to 2/5 with sustained effort. Upper extremity strength 5/5 throughout. During testing of right hip flexion, involuntary contralateral hip extension is noted (positive Hoover sign on the right). Intermittent whole-body tremor noted during examination, which is variable in amplitude and frequency, entrains to distraction testing, and diminishes when the patient is engaged in conversation.
neurological_exam.sensory: Reports decreased sensation to light touch and pinprick in the right leg in a non-dermatomal distribution, with a sharp midline split at the trunk. Vibration and proprioception intact bilaterally. No extinction on double simultaneous stimulation.
neurological_exam.reflexes: 2+ and symmetric at biceps, triceps, brachioradialis, patellar, and Achilles bilaterally. Plantar responses flexor (downgoing) bilaterally. No clonus.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose intact bilaterally without dysmetria. Heel-to-shin testing on the left is performed normally; on the right, the patient exhibits inconsistent performance with exaggerated wavering that does not follow a cerebellar pattern.
neurological_exam.gait: The patient demonstrates a markedly effortful gait with dragging of the right leg, but with inconsistent patterns — at times the leg appears to buckle yet the patient does not fall. When distracted by dual-task testing (counting backward from 100 by 7s), gait pattern transiently normalizes. Romberg testing shows dramatic swaying without falling, with improvement when patient is distracted.
neurological_exam.additional: During the examination, the patient had a witnessed episode of bilateral upper extremity shaking lasting approximately 90 seconds, with eyes tightly closed, side-to-side head movement, and preserved awareness (responded to name during event). The tremor increased in amplitude with attention and diminished when the examiner asked the patient to perform serial subtractions. No post-event confusion or drowsiness noted.

patient.chief_complaint: Intermittent whole-body tremor and episodes of unresponsiveness with shaking over the past 4 months, with progressive right leg weakness over the past 6 weeks
patient.history_present_illness:
Ms. Kowalski is a 35-year-old right-handed administrative assistant who presents to the neurology inpatient service after being transferred from an outside emergency department following two episodes of unresponsiveness with whole-body shaking in the span of 12 hours. She reports that the first episode occurred at work during a staff meeting. According to coworkers, she suddenly slumped in her chair and developed rhythmic shaking of both arms and legs lasting approximately two minutes. She was apparently responsive to her name being called during the event but seemed unable to speak. When the shaking stopped, she was tearful but alert, with no confusion or disorientation. She was taken to a local emergency department where vital signs and a basic metabolic panel were normal. She was discharged with outpatient neurology follow-up recommended.

Approximately eight hours later, at home with her partner, she experienced a second event. Her partner describes bilateral arm trembling and apparent unresponsiveness for roughly 90 seconds. Her eyes were noted to be tightly shut during the episode. She again recovered rapidly without post-event confusion, though she reported feeling emotionally overwhelmed. Her partner called emergency services and she was brought to the current hospital.

On further history, the patient describes having experienced similar episodes approximately six to eight times over the past four months. She reports that the first event occurred two weeks after learning that her position at work was being restructured, a period she characterizes as extremely stressful. The episodes vary in duration from 30 seconds to three minutes and occur without a clear consistent trigger, though she notes they seem more frequent during periods of heightened emotional distress. She denies tongue biting, urinary incontinence, or injury during these episodes. She has not been aware of any aura or warning symptom.

In addition to the episodic events, Ms. Kowalski reports progressive weakness in her right leg that began approximately six weeks ago. She first noticed difficulty climbing stairs, and the weakness has gradually worsened such that she now drags her right foot when walking. She denies any back pain, bowel or bladder dysfunction, or preceding trauma. She reports decreased sensation in the right leg but acknowledges that the numbness does not follow a consistent pattern and sometimes shifts location. She has been increasingly anxious about the possibility of a serious neurological condition such as multiple sclerosis, which her maternal aunt was diagnosed with at a similar age. She has been reading extensively about neurological diseases online, which has heightened her distress. Her sleep has been poor, with difficulty falling asleep and frequent nighttime awakenings. She denies any recent febrile illness, rash, joint symptoms, or visual changes.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 10 Hz alpha rhythm, symmetric and reactive to eye opening
background.sleep_features: Vertex waves and sleep spindles present; normal sleep architecture

findings.frequency: One event captured during the 4-hour recording
findings.morphology: During a witnessed clinical event consisting of bilateral upper extremity tremor and apparent unresponsiveness lasting 75 seconds, the EEG showed continuous normal background activity with well-formed posterior dominant rhythm. No ictal EEG correlate was identified. No electrographic seizure pattern, no rhythmic or periodic discharges, no post-event slowing.
findings.clinical_correlation: The dissociation between clinical symptoms (tremor, apparent unresponsiveness) and normal EEG activity is consistent with a psychogenic non-epileptic seizure (PNES) or functional seizure. This finding does NOT exclude the possibility of a coexistent epileptic seizure disorder if other event types exist.

artifacts.description: Prominent muscle and movement artifact during the clinical event, partially obscuring bilateral temporal channels. Despite artifact, posterior channels maintained adequate quality to confirm absence of ictal pattern.

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: 3 minutes of hyperventilation performed; no abnormalities elicited. Patient reported feeling lightheaded but no clinical event occurred.
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: Photic stimulation at 1-30 Hz performed; normal photic driving response. No photoparoxysmal response.

eeg.impression: Normal EEG with one clinical event captured. The witnessed clinical episode of bilateral upper extremity tremor with apparent unresponsiveness had NO electrographic correlate, consistent with a psychogenic non-epileptic seizure (PNES). No epileptiform discharges were identified. Normal background activity throughout.
eeg.limitations: Some movement artifact during the clinical event partially obscured temporal leads, though posterior channels remained interpretable.
recommended_actions: Clinical correlation with semiology and examination findings
Consider prolonged video-EEG monitoring if additional event types are reported

signal_characteristics.T2_FLAIR: No signal abnormality identified. No white matter lesions.

additional_observations: Hippocampi symmetric in size and signal bilaterally
Normal appearance of the basal ganglia, thalami, and brainstem
Major intracranial vessels demonstrate normal flow voids
mri.impression: Normal MRI of the brain without and with contrast. No evidence of demyelinating disease, structural lesion, stroke, or other intracranial pathology. Specifically, no findings to suggest multiple sclerosis.
recommended_actions: Correlate with clinical and electrodiagnostic findings
If spinal cord pathology is suspected, consider dedicated MRI of the cervical and thoracic spine

Additional.test: Prolactin (post-event, drawn 30 minutes after witnessed episode)
Additional.clinical_significance: Normal post-event prolactin. In epileptic generalized tonic-clonic seizures, prolactin typically rises 2-3x above baseline within 10-20 minutes. A normal level supports a non-epileptic etiology for the witnessed event.

labs.interpretation: All laboratory values within normal limits, including CBC, BMP, thyroid function, inflammatory markers, and vitamin levels. Post-event prolactin is normal, which argues against an epileptic seizure as the cause of the witnessed episode.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 10 Hz alpha rhythm, well-formed, symmetric, and reactive
background.symmetry: Symmetric without focal slowing or asymmetry
background.sleep_features: Normal sleep architecture with vertex waves, K-complexes, and sleep spindles

findings.type: Clinical event 1 - bilateral upper extremity tremor
findings.morphology: Patient reported feeling anxious; developed rhythmic bilateral arm shaking lasting 105 seconds with eyes tightly closed and side-to-side head movement. EEG showed continuous normal awake background with muscle artifact overlying the tracing. No ictal EEG correlate. Normal posterior dominant rhythm maintained throughout.
findings.clinical_correlation: Psychogenic non-epileptic seizure (PNES) / functional seizure. No EEG correlate.

findings.type: Clinical event 2 - whole-body shaking with apparent unresponsiveness
findings.morphology: Patient appeared unresponsive with whole-body shaking for approximately 2 minutes. Eyes were closed throughout. Staff noted patient could be redirected with verbal commands toward end of episode. EEG showed persistent normal background without any ictal pattern. No post-event slowing.
findings.clinical_correlation: Second captured event with no EEG correlate, further supporting diagnosis of PNES.

findings.type: Clinical event 3 - right leg weakness episode
findings.morphology: Patient reported acute worsening of right leg weakness. No EEG change observed. Normal background maintained.
findings.clinical_correlation: Transient symptom exacerbation without EEG correlate. Consistent with functional symptom.

artifacts.description: Significant movement artifact during clinical events, particularly affecting frontal and temporal channels. Posterior channels retained adequate quality for interpretation.

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Performed on day 1; no abnormalities. No clinical event provoked.
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: Normal photic driving; no photoparoxysmal response.
activating_procedures.sleep_deprivation: Relative sleep deprivation on night 1; no interictal epileptiform discharges emerged.

output.impression: Normal 48-hour continuous video-EEG monitoring. Three clinical events were captured, all without any electrographic correlate, confirming the diagnosis of psychogenic non-epileptic seizures (PNES). No interictal epileptiform discharges were identified. Normal background activity with preserved sleep architecture throughout the recording.
output.limitations: Movement artifact during events partially obscured some channels, but posterior channels maintained sufficient quality for confident interpretation.
recommended_actions: Communicate diagnosis of PNES to patient with supportive, non-dismissive approach
Referral to psychiatry or psychologist experienced in functional neurological disorders
Consider cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT) as evidence-based treatment for PNES

Nerve_Conduction_Studies_Sensory.test: Right superficial peroneal nerve - SNAP amplitude

output.interpretation: Normal electrodiagnostic study. Motor and sensory nerve conduction studies of the right lower and upper extremities are within normal limits. Needle EMG of the right lower extremity muscles and lumbar paraspinals shows no evidence of denervation, myopathy, or radiculopathy. These findings argue against peripheral neuropathy, motor neuron disease, myopathy, or lumbosacral radiculopathy as a cause of the patient's right leg weakness.

signal_characteristics.T2: Normal spinal cord signal throughout. No intramedullary signal abnormality.
signal_characteristics.post_contrast: No abnormal enhancement of the spinal cord or meninges

signal_characteristics.T2: Normal spinal cord signal. No intramedullary lesion identified.

additional_observations: Mild disc desiccation at C5-C6, likely degenerative, without significant disc herniation or canal stenosis
Conus medullaris terminates at the L1 level, normal position
No evidence of spinal cord compression or myelopathy
No leptomeningeal enhancement to suggest neurosarcoidosis or meningeal carcinomatosis
output.impression: Normal MRI of the cervical and thoracic spine with contrast. No evidence of demyelinating disease, myelopathy, structural cord lesion, or compressive pathology. Mild degenerative changes at C5-C6 without clinical significance. No findings to explain the patient's lower extremity weakness.
recommended_actions: Clinical correlation; imaging does not explain the reported symptoms
Consider functional etiology given normal structural imaging

Emotional_Functioning.reference_range: 0-4 minimal, 5-9 mild, 10-14 moderate, 15-19 moderately severe, 20-27 severe

Emotional_Functioning.clinical_significance: Score above threshold; suggests probable PTSD symptoms warranting further evaluation
Valid effort; no evidence of malingering

output.interpretation: Neuropsychological testing reveals intact cognitive functioning across all domains assessed, including attention, processing speed, memory, and executive function. No evidence of neurodegenerative or structural cognitive impairment. Emotional functioning measures reveal moderately severe depression (PHQ-9 = 16), moderate anxiety (GAD-7 = 14), and elevated PTSD symptom scores (PCL-5 = 38) above the clinical threshold. Symptom validity testing confirms adequate effort with no evidence of malingering. The psychological profile is consistent with significant psychiatric comorbidity that may contribute to or maintain functional neurological symptoms.

Psychiatric_Assessment.value: Functional Neurological Symptom Disorder (Conversion Disorder) with mixed symptoms (weakness and abnormal movements/seizures)
Psychiatric_Assessment.clinical_significance: F44.4 - Positive clinical signs (Hoover sign, entrainment) support diagnosis
Current PHQ-9 score 16; prior episode at age 28
GAD-7 score 14; longstanding anxiety predating current symptoms

Psychiatric_Assessment.value: Unspecified Trauma- and Stressor-Related Disorder, rule out PTSD
Psychiatric_Assessment.clinical_significance: Elevated PCL-5 score; patient reports difficult childhood but declines detailed disclosure at this time
Denies current suicidal ideation, intent, or plan. No prior attempts. Protective factors include supportive partner and engagement with treatment.

output.interpretation: Comprehensive psychiatric evaluation supports a diagnosis of Functional Neurological Symptom Disorder with significant psychiatric comorbidity including recurrent moderate depression, generalized anxiety, and possible trauma-related symptoms. The patient has identifiable psychosocial stressors (workplace restructuring, history of adverse childhood experiences) that temporally correlate with symptom onset and exacerbation. There is no evidence of malingering or factitious disorder; the patient appears genuinely distressed by her symptoms. Recommendations include psychoeducation about FND, cognitive behavioral therapy with a therapist experienced in FND, optimization of antidepressant therapy (consider increasing escitalopram or switching to an SNRI), and gradual reintroduction of physical activity through physiotherapy with an FND-informed approach.
abnormal_values_summary: Functional Neurological Symptom Disorder with mixed symptoms

output.interpretation: Comprehensive autoimmune and neuroimmunological panel is entirely negative. No serological evidence of systemic autoimmune disease, autoimmune encephalitis (NMDAR, LGI1, CASPR2), neuromyelitis optica (AQP4), MOG antibody disease, or neurosarcoidosis. These results further support a non-organic (functional) etiology for the patient's symptoms.

output.query: functional neurological disorder treatment evidence-based management PNES

results.title: Cognitive behavioural therapy for functional neurological symptoms: A systematic review and meta-analysis
results.summary: CBT is the most evidence-based psychological intervention for FND/PNES, with moderate effect sizes for seizure reduction and improvement in psychosocial functioning.

results.title: Diagnosis and management of functional neurological disorder
results.authors: Espay AJ, Aybek S, Carson A et al.
results.summary: FND should be diagnosed by positive clinical signs (Hoover sign, tremor entrainment, etc.), not by exclusion. A multidisciplinary approach combining neurology, psychiatry, physiotherapy, and psychology is recommended.

results.title: Physiotherapy for functional motor symptoms: A consensus recommendation
results.authors: Nielsen G, Stone J, Matthews A et al.
results.journal: Journal of Neurology Neurosurgery and Psychiatry
results.summary: Specialized physiotherapy using movement retraining, distraction techniques, and graded exercise is effective for functional motor symptoms including weakness and gait disorders.

output.summary: Evidence-based treatment for FND involves: (1) transparent communication of the diagnosis using positive clinical signs, (2) CBT as first-line psychological therapy, (3) specialized physiotherapy for motor symptoms, (4) treatment of psychiatric comorbidities, and (5) multidisciplinary coordination. Anti-epileptic drugs are NOT indicated for PNES and should be avoided.

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Functional neurological symptom disorder (conversion disorder) with mixed symptoms — psychogenic non-epileptic seizures and functional limb weakness

differential.key_features: Episodes lack EEG correlate during captured events, preserved awareness during events, eyes tightly closed, no post-ictal confusion, normal post-event prolactin. However, some overlap in semiology warrants video-EEG to definitively distinguish.

Patient's concern given family history (aunt with MS). However, brain MRI is normal with no white matter lesions, no optic neuritis, and spine MRI shows no demyelinating lesions. Age and sex demographics are compatible but clinical signs are positive for functional etiology.

Progressive leg weakness could suggest myelopathy, but spine MRI is normal, reflexes are normal (not hyperreflexic), and weakness pattern is non-anatomical with positive functional signs (Hoover sign, give-way pattern).

Leg weakness and sensory changes could suggest peripheral nerve pathology, but EMG/NCS is normal, sensory deficit does not follow a dermatomal pattern, and midline splitting is a positive functional sign.
Episodic behavioral changes could raise concern, but the patient has preserved cognition, normal MRI, normal EEG background, and negative autoimmune antibody panel.

optimal_actions.action: Perform detailed neurological examination looking for positive functional signs (Hoover sign, tremor entrainment, distractibility, give-way weakness, midline splitting of sensory loss)
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Positive Hoover sign, give-way weakness, entrainable tremor, non-dermatomal sensory loss with midline split — all positive indicators of functional neurological disorder

optimal_actions.action: Order routine EEG with attempt to capture a habitual event
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal EEG; if event captured, no electrographic correlate confirming PNES

optimal_actions.action: Order brain MRI with and without contrast to rule out structural pathology
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal brain MRI with no evidence of demyelination, mass, or stroke

optimal_actions.action: Order basic laboratory workup including CBC, BMP, thyroid function, and post-event prolactin
optimal_actions.expected_finding: All labs normal including post-event prolactin, arguing against epileptic seizure

optimal_actions.action: Order prolonged video-EEG monitoring to capture multiple habitual events and confirm absence of EEG correlate
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Multiple events captured without EEG correlate, confirming PNES diagnosis

optimal_actions.action: Consider spine MRI to rule out myelopathy given progressive leg weakness
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal cervical and thoracic spine MRI

optimal_actions.action: Consider EMG/NCS of the affected lower extremity to rule out peripheral nerve pathology
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal motor and sensory nerve conduction; normal EMG

optimal_actions.action: Communicate diagnosis to patient using a supportive, transparent approach emphasizing positive diagnostic signs rather than framing as exclusion of other diseases
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Patient understanding and acceptance of diagnosis, which is associated with better outcomes

optimal_actions.action: Refer for neuropsychological testing and psychiatric evaluation to assess comorbid conditions
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Identification of comorbid depression, anxiety, and possible trauma-related disorder requiring treatment

optimal_actions.action: Refer to specialized physiotherapy for functional motor symptoms and CBT for PNES
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Engagement in evidence-based treatment with anticipated gradual improvement

critical_actions: Identify positive functional neurological signs on examination (Hoover sign, tremor entrainment, give-way weakness) rather than diagnosing by exclusion alone
Obtain EEG (ideally video-EEG) to demonstrate absence of ictal correlate during a captured clinical event
Obtain brain MRI to exclude structural lesion
Check post-event prolactin to help differentiate PNES from epileptic seizure
Communicate the diagnosis in a supportive, non-dismissive manner using positive language
Refer for psychological/psychiatric evaluation and treatment (CBT)
Refer for specialized physiotherapy for functional motor symptoms
contraindicated_actions: Starting anti-epileptic drugs (AEDs) for presumed epileptic seizures without EEG confirmation — AEDs are not effective for PNES and expose the patient to unnecessary side effects
Dismissing symptoms as 'not real' or 'all in the patient's head' — this damages therapeutic alliance and worsens outcomes
Ordering excessive invasive procedures (brain biopsy, repeated lumbar punctures) when clinical signs positively support FND
Attributing diagnosis solely to exclusion ('we didn't find anything wrong') without demonstrating positive functional signs
Prescribing benzodiazepines as seizure abortive therapy for witnessed PNES events
Ordering cerebral angiography or invasive vascular studies in the absence of any imaging or clinical suggestion of vascular pathology
key_reasoning_points: FND should be diagnosed by positive clinical signs (Hoover sign, tremor entrainment, give-way weakness, non-dermatomal sensory loss with midline split), not merely by exclusion of other diagnoses
The dissociation between clinical seizure-like events and normal EEG activity is the hallmark of psychogenic non-epileptic seizures (PNES)
Normal post-event prolactin supports PNES over epileptic seizures, particularly for convulsive events

The patient's psychiatric comorbidity (depression, anxiety, possible trauma history) and psychosocial stressors are common co-occurring features of FND but are not required for diagnosis and do not 'explain' the symptoms in a reductive sense

Some features in this case overlap with epilepsy (episodes of apparent unresponsiveness with shaking), requiring video-EEG for definitive differentiation — this is what makes it a moderate difficulty case
Family history of MS and the patient's fear of MS create an important differential to explicitly evaluate and exclude with imaging
Symptom validity testing showing adequate effort argues against malingering, which is an important distinction from FND
Evidence-based treatment involves multidisciplinary care: neurology, psychiatry/psychology (CBT), and specialized physiotherapy

metadata.author: NeuroBench Generation Pipeline
clinical_pearls: Hoover sign: involuntary hip extension on the unaffected side when testing hip flexion on the affected side — a reliable positive sign of functional weakness
Tremor entrainment: functional tremor changes frequency to match or is disrupted by voluntary rhythmic movements of another limb
Eyes tightly closed during a seizure-like event is more common in PNES than in epileptic seizures
Midline splitting of sensory loss is a positive functional sign, as true spinal or peripheral lesions do not produce exact midline split
Post-event prolactin is typically elevated 2-3x baseline after generalized tonic-clonic seizures but remains normal after PNES
